description: Review key concepts in nerve and muscle physiology.

cards.answer: Synapse between motor neuron and skeletal muscle. Action potential -> Ca2+ entry -> ACh vesicle release -> ACh binds nicotinic receptors on motor end plate -> Na+ influx -> end plate potential -> muscle action potential -> contraction. ACh broken down by acetylcholinesterase.

cards.text: What is the sliding filament theory?
cards.answer: Muscle contraction occurs when actin (thin) filaments slide over myosin (thick) filaments. Myosin heads form cross-bridges with actin, powered by ATP hydrolysis. Ca2+ from sarcoplasmic reticulum removes tropomyosin block, allowing cross-bridge cycling. Sarcomere shortens.

tip.tip: Filaments slide, they don't shorten themselves.

cards.text: What is the difference between skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle?
cards.answer: Skeletal: striated, voluntary, multinucleated, no gap junctions. Cardiac: striated, involuntary, intercalated discs with gap junctions, autorhythmic. Smooth: non-striated, involuntary, single nucleus, gap junctions, slow sustained contraction.

In myelinated nerves, action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier (gaps in myelin sheath). Much faster than continuous conduction in unmyelinated fibres. Speed proportional to fibre diameter and myelination. Demyelination (MS) slows or blocks conduction.

Action potential travels along sarcolemma and down T-tubules -> activates voltage-sensitive DHP receptors -> opens ryanodine receptors on sarcoplasmic reticulum -> Ca2+ release into cytoplasm -> Ca2+ binds troponin C -> tropomyosin moves -> actin-myosin cross-bridge cycling.

tip.tip: The link between electrical signal and mechanical contraction.

cards.answer: One motor neuron and all the muscle fibres it innervates. Small motor units (eye muscles: 1 neuron: 5-10 fibres) for fine control. Large motor units (quadriceps: 1 neuron: 1000+ fibres) for power. Recruitment: small units first (Henneman size principle).

tip.tip: The functional unit of motor control.

cards.answer: Decline in muscle force during sustained activity. Central fatigue: reduced motor cortex drive. Peripheral fatigue: accumulation of metabolites (H+, Pi, ADP), depletion of glycogen, impaired Ca2+ release, failure of excitation-contraction coupling.

cards.text: What are the types of nerve fibres?
cards.answer: A-alpha: largest, fastest (proprioception, motor). A-beta: touch, pressure. A-gamma: muscle spindle motor. A-delta: fast pain, temperature. B: preganglionic autonomic. C: smallest, slowest, unmyelinated (slow pain, postganglionic sympathetic).
